abstract.para: These functions support Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed (ECEF) and Earth-Centered Inertial (ECI) coordinate frames for aerospace and satellite tracking applications. They provide frame conversion between ECEF (SRID 4978) and three ECI frames (ICRF, J2000, TEME), ECEF coordinate accessors suitable for index expressions, and Earth Orientation Parameter (EOP) infrastructure for high-accuracy transforms.
Provided by the postgis_ecef_eci extension, which requires the core postgis extension.

section.title: Installation and Upgrade
section.para: The ECEF/ECI functions are delivered as a separate PostgreSQL extension that depends on postgis. Install it after PostGIS:
Installation registers three ECI spatial reference systems in spatial_ref_sys (see ) and creates the postgis_eop table for Earth Orientation Parameters (see ).

section.title: ECI Spatial Reference Identifiers
section.para: The extension registers three Earth-Centered Inertial (ECI) coordinate reference systems in the user-defined SRID range. The SRIDs are inserted with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING so they will not overwrite any pre-existing user entries.

row.entry: International Celestial Reference Frame. IAU-recommended quasi-inertial frame tied to extragalactic radio sources.
J2000.0 epoch frame. Mean equator and equinox of 2000-01-01T12:00:00 TT.
True Equator, Mean Equinox. The native output frame of the SGP4/SDP4 orbital propagator.

section.para: The ECEF frame used by these functions is SRID 4978 (WGS 84 geocentric, also known as EPSG:4978). All coordinates are in metres along the X, Y, Z geocentric axes.
section.programlisting: -- Verify the registered SRIDs SELECT srid, auth_name, srtext FROM spatial_ref_sys WHERE srid IN (900001, 900002, 900003);

refnamediv.refpurpose: Converts a geometry from ECEF (SRID 4978) to an Earth-Centered Inertial frame.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Transforms a geometry from the ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) frame to an ECI (Earth-Centered Inertial) frame by applying the Earth rotation angle for the given epoch.
The input geometry must have SRID 4978 (WGS 84 geocentric). The returned geometry carries the ECI SRID corresponding to the requested frame (900001 for ICRF, 900002 for J2000, 900003 for TEME).
The frame parameter is case-insensitive and accepts 'ICRF', 'J2000', or 'TEME'. If omitted it defaults to 'ICRF'.
Because the result depends on the epoch (Earth rotation angle), the function is declared STABLE rather than IMMUTABLE. It is PARALLEL SAFE.
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Convert an ECEF point to ICRF at a specific epoch:
Convert to the TEME frame (for TLE/SGP4 workflows):
Round-trip conversion (ECEF to ECI and back):
refsection.programlisting: -- Round-trip: ECEF -> ICRF -> ECEF should return the original point WITH original AS ( SELECT ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(4510731, 4510731, 0), 4978) AS geom ), eci AS ( SELECT ST_ECEF_To_ECI(geom, '2024-01-01 00:00:00+00') AS geom FROM original ), roundtrip AS ( SELECT ST_ECI_To_ECEF(geom, '2024-01-01 00:00:00+00') AS geom FROM eci ) SELECT ST_AsText(o.geom) AS original, ST_AsText(r.geom) AS roundtrip, ST_Distance(o.geom, r.geom) AS error_metres FROM original o, roundtrip r; -- error_metres should be effectively zero (sub-millimetre)

refnamediv.refpurpose: Converts a geometry from an Earth-Centered Inertial frame back to ECEF (SRID 4978).

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Transforms a geometry from an ECI (Earth-Centered Inertial) frame back to ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) by applying the inverse Earth rotation angle for the given epoch.

The input geometry must carry an ECI SRID (900001, 900002, or 900003). The frame parameter is cross-validated against the input SRID: if the geometry has SRID 900003 but you pass 'ICRF', an error is raised. This prevents accidental misuse of ECI data from a different frame.
The returned geometry always has SRID 4978 (ECEF).
The frame parameter is case-insensitive and accepts 'ICRF', 'J2000', or 'TEME'. If omitted it defaults to 'ICRF'.
Declared STABLE and PARALLEL SAFE.
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Convert an ECI (ICRF) point back to ECEF:
SRID mismatch error -- the geometry is TEME (900003) but the frame parameter says ICRF:
refsection.programlisting: -- This raises an error because SRID 900003 does not match 'ICRF' SELECT ST_ECI_To_ECEF( ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(6000000, 0, 0), 900003), '2024-06-15 12:00:00+00'::timestamptz, 'ICRF' ); -- ERROR: ST_ECIToECEF: geometry SRID 900003 does not match frame 'ICRF' (SRID 900001)
refsection.para: Convert a batch of satellite positions from J2000 to ECEF:
refsection.programlisting: SELECT id, ST_ECI_To_ECEF(eci_position, obs_epoch, 'J2000') AS ecef_position FROM satellite_observations WHERE frame = 'J2000';

refnamediv.refpurpose: Converts a geometry from ECEF to ECI with automatic Earth Orientation Parameter (EOP) correction.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Transforms a geometry from ECEF to an ECI frame with enhanced precision using Earth Orientation Parameters (EOP). The function automatically looks up EOP data from the postgis_eop table via and applies:

listitem.para: dut1 correction: the Earth Rotation Angle is computed using UT1 time instead of UTC, eliminating up to ~0.9 seconds of rotation error (~460 m at the equator).
Polar motion (xp, yp): additional X-axis and Y-axis rotations of up to ~0.3 arcseconds (~10 m).

refsection.para: If the epoch falls outside the loaded EOP data range, the function falls back to the simplified ERA-only rotation used by .
The rotation convention follows IERS 2003 (simplified, without precession-nutation): ECI = Rz(+ERA_UT1) * Rx(-yp) * Ry(-xp) * ECEF
The input must have SRID 4978. The returned geometry carries the ECI SRID for the requested frame.
Declared STABLE and PARALLEL SAFE.
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Convert with automatic EOP lookup (requires loaded EOP data):
Compare EOP-enhanced vs standard result:

refnamediv.refpurpose: Converts a geometry from ECI to ECEF with automatic Earth Orientation Parameter (EOP) correction.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Transforms a geometry from an ECI frame back to ECEF with enhanced precision using Earth Orientation Parameters. This is the inverse of .
Applies the IERS 2003 convention: ECEF = Ry(xp) * Rx(yp) * Rz(-ERA_UT1) * ECI
EOP values are automatically interpolated from the postgis_eop table. Falls back to ERA-only rotation when EOP data is unavailable for the requested epoch.
The input must carry an ECI SRID (900001-900003). The returned geometry has SRID 4978 (ECEF).
Declared STABLE and PARALLEL SAFE.
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Round-trip with EOP correction:
refsection.programlisting: WITH original AS ( SELECT ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(5000000, 3000000, 4000000), 4978) AS geom, '2024-06-15 12:00:00+00'::timestamptz AS epoch ), eci AS ( SELECT ST_ECEF_To_ECI_EOP(geom, epoch) AS geom, epoch FROM original ), roundtrip AS ( SELECT ST_ECI_To_ECEF_EOP(geom, epoch) AS geom FROM eci ) SELECT ST_Distance(o.geom, r.geom) AS error_metres FROM original o, roundtrip r; -- error_metres should be effectively zero

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns the X coordinate (metres) of an ECEF point geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns the X coordinate of an ECEF point in metres. The input geometry must be a POINT with SRID 4978 (WGS 84 geocentric / ECEF). Returns NULL for empty geometries.

Unlike ST_X(), which is declared STABLE, ST_ECEF_X() is declared IMMUTABLE. This means it can be used in expression indexes, materialized view definitions, generated columns, and other contexts that require immutable functions.
The function is PARALLEL SAFE and STRICT (returns NULL on NULL input).
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Extract ECEF X coordinate:
Create an expression index on the X coordinate for fast range queries:
Use in a materialized view with precomputed coordinates:
refsection.programlisting: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW sat_ecef_coords AS SELECT id, ST_ECEF_X(geom) AS ecef_x, ST_ECEF_Y(geom) AS ecef_y, ST_ECEF_Z(geom) AS ecef_z FROM sat_positions;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns the Y coordinate (metres) of an ECEF point geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns the Y coordinate of an ECEF point in metres. The input geometry must be a POINT with SRID 4978 (WGS 84 geocentric / ECEF). Returns NULL for empty geometries.
Declared IMMUTABLE, PARALLEL SAFE, STRICT. Safe for use in expression indexes, materialized views, and generated columns.
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Extract ECEF Y coordinate:

refnamediv.refpurpose: Returns the Z coordinate (metres) of an ECEF point geometry.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns the Z coordinate of an ECEF point in metres. The input geometry must be a POINT with SRID 4978 (WGS 84 geocentric / ECEF) and must have a Z dimension. Returns NULL for empty geometries or geometries without a Z dimension.
Declared IMMUTABLE, PARALLEL SAFE, STRICT. Safe for use in expression indexes, materialized views, and generated columns.
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Extract ECEF Z coordinate:
Use all three accessors together for generated columns:

section.title: Earth Orientation Parameters (EOP)
section.para: High-accuracy ECEF-to-ECI transforms require Earth Orientation Parameters published by the International Earth Rotation and Reference Systems Service (IERS) in their Bulletin A. The extension provides a storage table, a loader function, and an interpolation function for working with this data.

section.title: postgis_eop Table
section.para: Created automatically when the extension is installed.
section.programlisting: CREATE TABLE postgis_eop ( mjd FLOAT8 PRIMARY KEY, -- Modified Julian Date xp FLOAT8 CHECK (xp BETWEEN -0.000278 AND 0.000278), -- pole x (arcsec) yp FLOAT8 CHECK (yp BETWEEN -0.000278 AND 0.000278), -- pole y (arcsec) dut1 FLOAT8 CHECK (dut1 BETWEEN -1.0 AND 1.0), -- UT1-UTC (seconds) dx FLOAT8, -- celestial pole offset dX (arcsec) dy FLOAT8 -- celestial pole offset dY (arcsec) );
section.para: Column descriptions:

listitem.para: mjd -- Modified Julian Date, the primary key. One row per day.
xp, yp -- Polar motion coordinates in arcseconds. CHECK constraints enforce the physically plausible range of +/- 1 arcsecond (0.000278 degrees).
dut1 -- Difference UT1-UTC in seconds. Constrained to +/- 1 second (leap-second window).
dx, dy -- Celestial pole offsets in arcseconds. May be NULL when not available in the source data.

refnamediv.refpurpose: Loads Earth Orientation Parameters from IERS Bulletin A fixed-width format text.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Parses the data argument as IERS Bulletin A fixed-width format text. Each valid line is parsed for Modified Julian Date (columns 19-27), polar motion x (columns 35-43), polar motion y (columns 44-52), and UT1-UTC (columns 59-68). Lines that are too short or fail to parse are silently skipped.

Parsed rows are upserted into the postgis_eop table using INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (mjd) DO UPDATE, so calling the function with overlapping data is safe and will update existing rows with newer values.
Returns the number of rows successfully loaded.
Declared VOLATILE (modifies table data).
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Load EOP data from a file using pg_read_file() (requires superuser or pg_read_server_files role):
refsection.programlisting: SELECT postgis_eop_load(pg_read_file('/tmp/finals2000A.data')); -- Returns: 365 (number of rows loaded)
refsection.para: Load EOP data supplied as a literal string (useful for testing):
Verify loaded data:
refsection.programlisting: SELECT mjd, xp, yp, dut1 FROM postgis_eop ORDER BY mjd LIMIT 5;

refnamediv.refpurpose: Interpolates Earth Orientation Parameters for a given epoch.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Converts the epoch timestamp to a Modified Julian Date, finds the two bracketing rows in postgis_eop, and performs linear interpolation to return the EOP values at the requested instant.
Returns a single row with columns: xp (float8), yp (float8), dut1 (float8), dx (float8), dy (float8).

Returns no rows (empty set) if the epoch is outside the date range of the loaded EOP data. If the epoch falls exactly on a stored MJD, the values from that row are returned directly without interpolation.
The dx and dy columns use COALESCE(..., 0) during interpolation, so NULL values in the source data are treated as zero.
Declared STABLE (reads but does not modify the EOP table).
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Interpolate EOP for a specific epoch:
Check whether EOP data covers a particular date (returns no rows if not):

refnamediv.refpurpose: Placeholder procedure for scheduling periodic EOP data refresh.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: A placeholder procedure intended to be connected to an external scheduler such as pg_cron for automated fetching of updated IERS Bulletin A data. In its current form it simply raises a NOTICE reminding the user to configure an external data-fetching mechanism.
A typical production setup would use pg_cron to periodically download Bulletin A data and call postgis_eop_load():
Availability: 3.5.0

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: Call the placeholder directly:

section.title: Hardware Acceleration
section.para: PostGIS can accelerate coordinate transforms using CPU SIMD instructions and GPU compute when available. Acceleration is automatic: the system detects hardware capabilities at startup and selects the optimal code path.

section.title: SIMD Acceleration
section.para: SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) acceleration processes multiple coordinate points in parallel using CPU vector instructions:

listitem.para: AVX2+FMA: 4-wide double precision (Intel Haswell+, AMD Zen+)
AVX-512: 8-wide double precision (Intel Skylake-X+, AMD Zen 4+)

section.para: SIMD acceleration has zero dispatch overhead and is always used when available. Build with --disable-simd to force scalar-only code paths.
Configure flags:
section.programlisting: ./configure --enable-avx2 # Enable AVX2 (auto-detected by default) ./configure --enable-avx512 # Enable AVX-512 (auto-detected by default) ./configure --disable-simd # Disable all SIMD, portable scalar only

section.title: GPU Acceleration
section.para: For large coordinate arrays, PostGIS can dispatch transforms to GPU hardware. Three GPU backends are supported:
Configure flags:

GPU dispatch is controlled by a point-count threshold. Arrays smaller than the threshold use CPU SIMD; larger arrays are sent to the GPU. By default (threshold = 0), PostGIS auto-calibrates on the first GPU dispatch by benchmarking GPU vs CPU SIMD at several point counts (1K, 10K, 100K, 1M) and selecting the crossover. This runs once per backend session and adds a one-time latency of approximately 1-2 seconds.
GUC parameters:
Typical crossover points by GPU class:

listitem.para: Data-centre GPUs (V100, A100, H100): 10K-100K points — high memory bandwidth makes GPU worthwhile at moderate sizes.
Workstation GPUs (RTX A2000/A4000): 100K-1M points — dispatch overhead is significant relative to compute throughput.
Laptop GPUs (RTX A2000 Laptop): GPU may never beat AVX2 SIMD — auto-calibration will set a very high threshold effectively disabling GPU dispatch.

section.title: Valkey GPU Batching
section.para: For streaming workloads (many small queries), Valkey-based batching accumulates transform requests and dispatches them as GPU-sized batches. This amortizes GPU dispatch overhead across multiple concurrent clients.
GUC parameters:
Requires: ./configure --with-valkey and a running Valkey/Redis instance.

section.title: Feature Detection
section.para: Use postgis_accel_features() to see detected acceleration capabilities and the current GPU dispatch threshold:
section.programlisting: SELECT postgis_accel_features(); -- Before first GPU use (auto-calibrate pending): -- "SIMD: AVX2+FMA; GPU: NVIDIA RTX A2000 8GB (compute 8.6, threshold: auto); Valkey: disabled" -- -- After auto-calibration: -- "SIMD: AVX2+FMA; GPU: NVIDIA RTX A2000 8GB (compute 8.6, threshold: 100000 pts); Valkey: disabled" -- -- No GPU available: -- "SIMD: AVX2+FMA; GPU: none; Valkey: disabled" -- -- ARM system: -- "SIMD: ARM NEON; GPU: none; Valkey: disabled"

section.title: Benchmark Results
section.para: Measured on x86_64 (gcc 12.2, -O2) with AVX2+FMA enabled. Throughput in millions of points per second:

Peak throughput is ~1.4 billion pts/sec (AVX2) at 10K points, limited by L2 cache at larger sizes. Numerical accuracy: max difference between scalar and AVX2+FMA is 9.31e-10 (sub-nanometer at Earth radius), caused by FMA rounding.

refsection.title: Description
refsection.para: Returns a text string describing detected SIMD instruction set (AVX2/AVX-512/ARM NEON/none), GPU backend (CUDA/ROCm/oneAPI/none with device name and dispatch threshold), and Valkey batching status (enabled/disabled).

When a GPU is detected, the threshold field shows either auto (calibration pending) or the calibrated/configured point count. Use SET postgis.gpu_dispatch_threshold = 0 to reset to auto-calibration mode.

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.programlisting: SELECT postgis_accel_features(); -- "SIMD: AVX2+FMA; GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 (compute 8.9, threshold: 10000 pts); Valkey: disabled" -- "SIMD: ARM NEON; GPU: none; Valkey: disabled" -- "SIMD: none; GPU: none; Valkey: disabled"
